stories.headline: Iran-Israel war reignites on 100th day: Israel hits four Iranian cities, Iran fires ~30 missiles in 'Operation Nasr'
stories.summary: On the war's 100th day, Israel struck military and radar sites in Tehran, Isfahan, Karaj and Tabriz after Iran fired roughly 30 ballistic missiles — its first direct barrage since the April 8 truce — in three waves the IRGC dubbed 'Operation Nasr.' Two Houthi missiles joined the salvos, and an Iranian strike reportedly hit a Mahshahr petrochemical plant. Trump phoned Netanyahu urging restraint, then publicly demanded both sides 'immediately stop shooting.' Brent crude jumped about 5% toward $97.

perspectives.keyFraming: A fragile, repeatedly violated ceasefire has collapsed as Iran's first direct fire on Israel since April triggers Israeli strikes back on Iran.
perspectives.summary: Israel said Iran launched missiles in the first bombardment since the early-April ceasefire; Iran's state broadcaster confirmed the launches and warned attacks would continue unless Israel halted operations in Lebanon. Israel then struck military targets in western and central Iran early June 8. Trump pressed both sides to stop firing and return to talks.

sources.title: Israel and Iran trade strikes, threatening to drag region back to full-scale war
The Latest: Israel and Iran trade fire in most serious confrontation since April truce

perspectives.keyFraming: IRGC's 'Operation Nasr' struck Israeli airbases in response to Israeli 'aggression'; Iran warns of far more devastating blows if Israel retaliates.
perspectives.summary: Iranian state media said the IRGC Aerospace Force launched Operation Nasr (Victory) against Israel's Nevatim and Tel Nof airbases in retaliation for Israeli strikes on radar sites across three parts of Iran. Military spokesman Ebrahim Zolfaghari warned that any Israeli retaliation, or an expansion of operations in southern Lebanon, would meet 'even more devastating blows.'

sources.title: IRGC Launches Operation Nasr Targeting Israel's Tel-Nof, Nevatim Airbases
Iran threatens even more massive strikes if Israel retaliates — military command

stories.headline: Brent jumps above $97 as renewed Iran-Israel strikes revive the war premium
stories.summary: Oil surged Monday June 8 after Iran and Israel traded direct strikes over the weekend, reversing a two-session slide driven by weak Chinese demand. Brent rose about 4.9% toward $97.7 a barrel and WTI about 3.4% toward $93.6, with an Iranian missile reportedly hitting a Mahshahr petrochemical plant. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed, and OPEC+'s fourth consecutive quota hike does little while Gulf barrels can't physically reach customers.

perspectives.keyFraming: A geopolitical shock reverses a demand-driven slide, as a fresh war premium is priced back into crude.
perspectives.summary: Brent and WTI rebounded sharply after Sunday's missile exchange between Iran and Israel — Tehran's first barrage since the early-April ceasefire — threatening the diplomatic track. The jump unwound Friday's drop to about $93 on weak Chinese imports. Analysts note OPEC+ raised paper quotas it cannot fill while Hormuz stays shut, leaving supply tight.

sources.title: Fragile ceasefire in jeopardy as Iran reportedly fires first missiles at Israel
OPEC+ approves fourth oil output quota hike since Strait of Hormuz closure

stories.headline: Schumer presses Senate floor vote on Iran war powers as ceasefire collapses on war's 100th day
stories.summary: With the fragile US-Iran ceasefire collapsing June 7-8 — the heaviest Israel-Iran fire since the April truce — Schumer is moving to force another Senate war-powers vote, vowing votes 'every week' until the war ends. The Senate had for the first time advanced such a resolution, 50-47, after four Republicans (Collins, Murkowski, Paul, Cassidy) defected; the House passed its own 215-208. The measure lacks the force of law and faces a likely Trump veto.

perspectives.keyFraming: A reckless, unpopular 'war of choice' Congress must end — and Republicans are finally cracking.
perspectives.summary: Democrats frame the renewed push as vindication: with the ceasefire collapsing, Schumer says Congress must seize the moment to stop the war before fighting fully resumes, pledging weekly votes. They tout the first-ever Senate procedural advance and growing GOP defections amid economic fallout and sliding Trump approval.

sources.title: 3 GOP senators break with leadership, vote to advance resolution to end Iran war

stories.headline: Federal judges who ruled against Trump describe surge in death threats in '60 Minutes' report
stories.summary: A June 7 '60 Minutes' segment featured 26 federal judges — 17 Republican and 9 Democratic appointees — describing feeling 'under siege' amid escalating threats after President Trump's attacks on the judiciary. Reagan appointee Judge John Coughenour, who blocked Trump's birthright-citizenship order, said he got hundreds of death threats; Judge Esther Salas, whose son was murdered in 2020, said she is more worried now. US Marshals are probing 'pizza doxxing' of judges' homes.

perspectives.keyFraming: Trump's attacks on judges are fueling a violent climate that threatens judicial independence.
perspectives.summary: The segment is framed as judges sounding the alarm: serious threats against judges jumped sharply over four years, with hundreds targeted last year. Coughenour and Salas describe death threats and intimidation tactics like 'pizza doxxing' tied to Trump's pattern of railing against adverse rulings.

sources.title: '60 Minutes' Puts on Judges to Sound the Alarm on Trump Attacks

perspectives.keyFraming: A bipartisan group of judges details threats; the White House calls the link to Trump 'deeply unserious.'
perspectives.summary: Coverage balances the judges' accounts — both Republican and Democratic appointees — against the White House response calling it 'deeply unserious' to suggest Trump's comments lead to threats. It notes the US Marshals investigation and the 2020 attack on Judge Salas's family.

sources.title: Federal judges who've ruled against Trump administration denounce threats against themselves, their families
White House says it's 'deeply unserious' to suggest Trump comments on judges may lead to threats
